coredata.dc:title: Investigation the cytotoxicity and photo-induced toxicity of carbon dot on yeast cell
coredata.dc:creator: Bagheri, Zeinab
Ehtesabi, Hamide
Hallaji, Zahra
Latifi, Hamid
Behroodi, Ebrahim
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a new fluorescent material with excellent water solubility, chemical inertness, and easy surface modification are a good candidate for bioimaging and biosensing due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Although carbon is not an intrinsically toxic substance, carbon nanomaterials such as CDs may cause risks to human health and the potentially hazardous effects of CDs on various living systems must be completely determined. So far, cytotoxicity studies of CDs have focused on human cells and are mainly conducted on limited cell lines. In the present study, toxicity assessment of CDs was evaluated on yeast cells Pichia pastoris as a unicellular eukaryotic model. Results revealed dose-dependent toxicity of CDs on yeast cells and less relative cell growth in 25 mg/ml of CDs as compared to the control group. CDs binding curve confirmed the interaction between CDs and surface of yeast cells. SEM images showed that the CDs caused cell shrinkage and hole formation on the surface of yeast cells and also induced slightly cell deformation. It was demonstrated that CDs could generate the ROS dose-dependently. Finally, results showed the growth inhibition and ROS generation effects of CDs were enhanced at light exposure, as an important environmental factor. These findings could have important implications for applications of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cytotoxicity
Photo induced-toxicity
Carbon dots
Yeast cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cultivation of yeast cells and toxicity testing
Investigation the interaction of CDs with surface of yeast cell
Assessment the photo-induced toxicity of CDs
Toxicity evaluation of CDs on yeast cell growth
interaction of CDs with surface of yeast cell
Measuring intracellular ROS in the presence and absence of light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CDs characterization: (a) OD, PL, and PLE of CDs (inset: a solution of CDs under visible light and UV radiation), (b) emission of CDs at different excitation wavelengths, (c) DLS diagram, and (d) TEM image of prepared CDs.

Toxicity evaluation of CDs on yeast cells. a) relative cell growth of co-incubation of yeast cells with different concentrations of CDs. b) Result of mitochondrial activity of yeast cells using MTT assay after incubation with different concentrations of CDs. in a and b, Data are shown as mean ± standard error of three different experiments (n = 3). *, ** and *** indicate significant differences from the control (no treatment) at p < 0.5, p < 0.01 and p < 0.001, respectively.

Carbon dot binding curves to P. pastoris. The curves depict the relative levels of fluorescence emission (excitation at 360 nm) induced by increasing concentrations of the CDs in the yeast suspensions. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). Significant difference from control was defined at ** p < 0.01 and *** p < 0.001. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

Effect of CDs on the morphology and damage of cell wall of P. pastoris. The SEM image of: (a, b) the un-treated cells and (c, d) The treated cells. the highlighted scale bar in a and c represents 5 µm, and in b an d represents 2 µm.

Photo-induced toxicity of prepared CDs on yeast cells at different light exposure time. Significant decrease of growth rate was observed for > 30 min of exposure time and > 15 mg/ml of CDs concentration. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). Significant difference from control (CDs Concentration and exposure time of 0) was defined at *p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001.

Effect of different CDs concentrations on intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) of yeast cells at different light exposure times. Data are shown as mean ± standard error from three different experiments (n = 3). p < 0.001.Significant difference from control (CDs Concentration and exposure time of 0) was defined at *p < 0.0,**p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001.

head.ce:title: Investigation the cytotoxicity and photo-induced toxicity of carbon dot on yeast cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Velenjak, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University G.C

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Velenjak, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University G.C

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laser & Plasma Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Velenjak, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laser & Plasma Research Institute, Shahid Beheshti University G.C

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a new fluorescent material with excellent water solubility, chemical inertness, and easy surface modification are a good candidate for bioimaging and biosensing due to their low toxicity and good biocompatibility. Although carbon is not an intrinsically toxic substance, carbon nanomaterials such as CDs may cause risks to human health and the potentially hazardous effects of CDs on various living systems must be completely determined. So far, cytotoxicity studies of CDs have focused on human cells and are mainly conducted on limited cell lines. In the present study, toxicity assessment of CDs was evaluated on yeast cells Pichia pastoris as a unicellular eukaryotic model. Results revealed dose-dependent toxicity of CDs on yeast cells and less relative cell growth in 25 mg/ml of CDs as compared to the control group. CDs binding curve confirmed the interaction between CDs and surface of yeast cells. SEM images showed that the CDs caused cell shrinkage and hole formation on the surface of yeast cells and also induced slightly cell deformation. It was demonstrated that CDs could generate the ROS dose-dependently. Finally, results showed the growth inhibition and ROS generation effects of CDs were enhanced at light exposure, as an important environmental factor. These findings could have important implications for applications of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots a new class of graphene nanoparticle have attracted great attention of many research groups due to their unique properties, including high photoluminescence, well solubility, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility that are widely used in bioimaging, drug delivery and theranostic (Hu et al., 2014; Ostadhossein and Pan, 2017; Zheng et al., 2015; Zhu et al., 2015). Apart from biomedical applications, CDs possess several interesting features, such as resistance to photobleaching, large surface area, wavelength-dependent emission, up-conversion capability and ease of synthesis, makes them promising materials in many industrial fields as well (Cayuela et al., 2016; Hola et al., 2014; Shen et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2016b; Wang et al., 2015a,2015b). While considering the new uses of CDs in wide range of novel applications such as LED fabrication, photocatalysis, supercapacitors and batteries, cause the entry and spread of the CDs to the environment (Wang et al., 2016c). Although carbon is not an intrinsically toxic substance, the specific material and structural configuration of carbon nanomaterials may cause risks to human health and the potentially hazardous effects on various living systems must be completely determined (Feng and Liu, 2011; Seabra et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2007, 2015).

Cytotoxicity studies of CDs have focused on human cells and are mainly conducted on limited cell lines, such as Hela cells, which restricts the generalization of the results to other cells, this could be performed using cells and simpler organisms (Dhawan and Sharma, 2010; Li et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2016a). Unicellular eukaryotic model organisms are widely used to study the toxicity of chemicals such as heavy metals, anticancer drugs, herbicides and food preservatives because of similarities of their biochemical structure, molecular mechanisms and cellular organization to those of higher organisms. According to our knowledge, there is no report has been conducted to investigate the toxicity effect of CDs on yeast cells (Wang et al., 2016a; Willaert et al., 2016).

Additionally, the enormous variety of synthesized CDs with diverse physicochemical properties, is the next challenge to explore the toxicity of CDs (Havrdova et al., 2016; Jeon et al., 2017). One approach to overcome this challenge is trying to understand the mechanism of CDs toxicity at the cellular level (Seabra et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2015a,2015b). several studies demonstrated that various CDs cause ROS generation on account of their surface chemistry and edge effect (Wang et al., 2016a). Wang et al. reported that the CDs-induced release of ROS caused DNA damage in fibroblast cells (NIH/3T3) (Wang et al., 2015a, 2015b). Jiang et al. also showed that the green fluorescent CD synthesized from GO through one-step hydrothermal method caused the ROS generation in HeLa cells dose-dependently (Jiang et al., 2015). Another recent study on NIH/3T3 cells, also demonstrated that the contribution of the ROS generation to the toxicity of CDs is dominantly related to the property of the nanomaterial itself, not to the properties of coating polymers (Havrdova et al., 2016). Additionally, Chong et al. demonstrated that CDs in the absence of light can efficiently scavenge a number of free radicals. However, upon exposure to blue light, exhibit significant phototoxicity through increasing intracellular ROS levels and reducing cell viability (Wu et al., 2013).

The objective of this study was to evaluate the potential hazards of CDs nanoparticle using the yeast Pichia pastoris as a unicellular eukaryotic model. Water-soluble fluorescent CDs were prepared from citric acid using pyrolysis method and then characterized by UV–vis spectroscopy, spectrofluorimetry, Dynamic light scattering and transmission electron microscopy. In the next step, the relative cell growth of yeast cells was assessed in the presence of different concentration of CDs. The spectrofluorometry was used to answer the question of whether the interaction between CDs and yeast cells and or CDs could adsorb on the cell surface. Additionally, the morphological changes of yeast treated with CDs were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Because the light is the important environmental factor, its impact on toxicity of CDs was also determined. Finally, the intercellular ROS generation induced CDs was examined. The finding of this study could be used for further application of CDs in biological systems.

Anhydrous citric acid, sodium hydroxide, yeast extract, soy peptone, D-glucose, agar, glutaraldehyde, sulfuric acid, and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), osmium tetra oxide, quinine sulphite and 2′,7′ dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (GmbH Munich, Germany). Ethanol and butanol were purchased from Mojallali (Tehran, Iran).

CDs were prepared using a citric acid pyrolysis method following our previous report (Bagheri et al., 2017). Briefly, 1 gr of anhydrous citric acid was heated to 160 °C for 50 min and then neutralized to pH of 7.0 by adding sodium hydroxide (NaOH) aqueous solution (0.5 M) under continuous stirring. The stock solution of CDs was stored at 4 °C until use. Optical properties of the CDs solution were determined using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (LAMBDA 950 UV/Vis/NIR Spectrophotometer, PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were obtained using a spectrophotometer (Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, Agilent, USA). Quantum yield (QY) of prepared CDs was measured using a quinine sulphite standard solution (quinine sulphite dissolved in 0.05 M solution of H2SO4). The particle size distribution of CDs was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Nanophox, Sympatec GmbH, Germany). The general morphology of prepared CDs was characterized by a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Zeiss – EM10C, Germany) operating at 80 kV. For TEM specimen preparation, the stock solution of CDs was diluted in water and sonicated for 10 min using an ultrasonic cleaner (Misonix– S3000, USA). Then a small drop of that was placed onto a copper grid supported with carbon film and dried at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cultivation of yeast cells and toxicity testing
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the toxicity effect of CDs on yeast cells, the biomass increase of treated yeast with various concentrations of CDs were compared to control. The yeast biomass was evaluated by the optical density (OD) measurement of the cell suspensions at 600 nm (Kasemets et al., 2012). P.pastoris wild type was grown on yeast peptone dextrose (YPD) medium (1% yeast extract/ 2% peptone / 2% dextrose). One ml aliquots of the overnight cultures of yeast cell suspension and 1 ml of different concentrations of CDs were added into the tubes, obtaining 2 ml mixtures containing yeast cells with an OD600 of 0.1 and CDs with the following concentrations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.1, 6.3, 12.5 and 25 mg/ml. The mixtures were cultured at 30 °C with stirring at 200 rpm for 16 h and then the absorbance were recorded at 600 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate; the relative cell growth is the ratio of cell number in treated sample to untreated control. Results were expressed as mean±SD of triplicate analyses.

Pichia pastoris wild type was grown on yeast peptone dextrose (YPD) medium; 1% yeast extract, 2% peptone, 2% dextrose at 30 °C. 1 ml aliquots of the overnight cultures of yeast cell suspension and 1 ml of different concentrations of CDs were added into the tubes, obtaining 2 ml mixtures containing yeast cells with an OD600 of 0.1 and CDs with the following concentrations: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 1.6, 3.1, 6.3, 12.5 and 25 mg/ml.

For better understanding of the toxicity effect of CDs on yeast cells, Cell proliferation and their mitochondrial activity were investigated by dimethylthiazol-2-y1–2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay (Rahimifard et al., 2014). In brief, CDs-treated cells were exposed to 50 μl MTT reagent (0.5 mg/ml) for 3 h at 37 °C. Then, purple formazan crystal, which appeared by reducing MTT in living cells, was dissolved by adding 150 μl dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to each samples. After 30 min shaking, the absorbance intensity was assessed at 570 nm using an ELISA reader (Biotech, Winooski, VT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation the interaction of CDs with surface of yeast cell
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the possibility of CDs binding and interaction to the cell surface, yeast cells were incubated with different concentration of CDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 mg/ml) for 3 h at 30 °C, while slowly shaking. The OD of cells in the yeast/CDs suspensions was 0.1. After incubation, the yeast cells were centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm and the pellet washed twice with sterilized PBS to remove unbound CDs. In the next step, the cell pellet was collected and resuspended in 3 ml of PBS under gentle vortex. Finally, the emission intensity of cell-treated CDs was recorded using a spectrofluorometer with excitation and emission wavelengths were 360 and 460 nm, respectively (Verma and Stellacci, 2010).

The morphological changes of P.pastoris treated with CDs were demonstrated using SEM (HITACHI S-4160). For this purpose, 10 ml of 25 mg/ml CDs solution was added into 10 ml of 2 × YPD medium. 500 μl of yeast suspension at OD600 0.1 was then added into the tube. The yeast/CDs suspensions were kept at 30 °C for 16 h with an agitation of 200 rpm in a dark incubator chamber. In addition, a sample was cultivated with the same conditions without CDs, as a test of control. After the incubation, cells from both tubes were harvested by light centrifugation and fixed by 2% glutaraldehyde (in 0.1 M Na-cacodylate buffer) for 3 h, and then were post-fixed in the same buffer with 1% osmium tetroxide for 1 h. Next, samples were dehydrated through a graded series of ethanol (30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100%) for 10 min for each ethanol concentration and followed by centrifugation. After discarding the solvent, samples were applied on to the frozen cover slip, dried and coated with gold-coater for 5 min (Ali et al., 2016; Khan et al., 2017; Nasrollahi et al., 2011; Syaefudin et al., 2014).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment the photo-induced toxicity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mixtures of 0.1 OD600 yeast cells and different concentrations of CDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 mg/ml) were prepared and exposed to white light under different exposure times (0, 15, 30, 45 and 60 min). Then, the yeast cell suspensions ware cultivated in dark condition for 16 h at 30 °C. Finally, the relative cell growth was calculated for each sample.

Mixtures of 0.1 OD600 yeast cells and different concentrations of CDs (0, 5, 15 and 25 mg/ml) were prepared and exposed to white light under different exposure times (0, 30 and 60 min). Then, the yeast cell suspensions ware cultivated in dark condition for 16 h at 30 °C. In the next step, cells were gently homogenized in 20 ml of the extraction buffer and centrifuged. The supernatant was used for measuring ROS formation and protein content. The intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) of the cells were determined using 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA). Briefly, 10 μl of DCFH-DA stock solution and 162 μl assay buffer were added to 50 μl of samples and incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. DCFH-DA deacetylated and converted to DCFH by cellular esterase. Then, cytosolic ROS oxidized this compound to 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Finally, the changes in emission at 488 nm excitation and 525 nm emission were detected using 96-well microplate fluorimeter every 1 min up to 60 min. Furthermore, protein content of the samples was investigated using Bradford reagent (Baeeri et al., 2018).

At least three independent experiments were carried out for each condition. Data were presented as mean ± SEM. One-way ANOVA and Tukey's multi-comparison tests were carried out using SPSS software (Version 23.0) to evaluate statistical differences. The p value of 0.01 was considered significant (Rahimifard et al., 2015).

Fig. 1 shows the characteristic analysis of the synthesized CDs. The UV–vis spectrum of the synthesized CDs is presented in Fig. 1(a) which shows a maximum absorption wavelength at 360 nm. The PL intensity diagram of CDs, with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, shows blue emission wavelength of 460 nm. The FWHM below 100 nm admits a homogeneity in size distribution of the nanoparticles (Sun and Lei, 2017; S. Wang et al., 2016b). Fig. 1(b) shows that the emission wavelength of the synthesized CDs is independent from the excitation wavelength. The mean diameter of the CDs was obtained to be 3.1 ± 0.4 nm from DLS result which is presented in Fig. 1(c). The narrow peak of DLS result shows a rather uniform size distribution in the synthesized CDs. According to TEM results, Fig. 1(d), the average diameter of the synthesized CDs is 3 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity evaluation of CDs on yeast cell growth
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the effect of CDs concentration on yeast cells, cells were treated to a linear concentration of CDs (0–25 mg/ml) and the growth inhibitory effect of CDs was determined using OD measurements ( Fig. 2(a)). Measurements showed no significant cell growth inhibition at concentrations of CDs lower than and equal to 5 mg/ml. CDs at concentrations higher than 10 mg/ml reduced cell growth dose-dependently (p < 0.01). The largest decrease in the yeast cell growth occurred at ∼ 25 mg/ml concentration of CDs at which cell growth was 76 ± 5% of the control sample (no treatment). Toxicity of CDs can be further confirmed by MTT assay (Dhawan and Sharma, 2010). Fig. 2(b) depicts the percent of mitochondrial activity of yeast cells in the absence and presence of CDs. As it's clear, All the studied concentrations of the CDs showed significant decrease in mitochondrial activity and this reduction is dose dependent. In addition, less activity of mitochondria was detected in 25 mg/ml of CDs as compared to the control group (p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: interaction of CDs with surface of yeast cell
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction of CDs with the surface of yeast cells was determined by recording the emission intensities of the adsorbed CDs on the cell surface. Fig. 3 shows the CDs intensity emission at 460 nm. Increasing the CDs concentration increased the emitted light intensity, indicating binding of CDs to the surface of yeast cells. the initial fluorescence increase due to binding of the fluorescent CDs to the cells, the fluorescence intensities reach plateaus – reflecting maximal available binding sites for the CD upon the cell surface.

SEM has been widely used in microbiology due to produce high resolution images of cellular components. In this study, the impact of CDs on physical structure and morphology of the yeast cells were evaluated through SEM and shown in
Fig. 5. The untreated cells (Fig. 5(a) and Fig. 5(b)) have well-defined shape (rounded or oval) with normal smooth surfaces. While in treated cells (Fig. 5(c) and Fig. 5(d)) cellular shrinkage, cellular deformation and percolation of the cell surface can be seen. The observed holes on the cell surface may be indicated the damage to the cell wall and entry of CDs into the cell (Ali et al., 2016; Willaert et al., 2016).

In order to show the effect of light on CDs toxicity, the CDs treated yeast cells were exposed to light for different time exposure. As shown in Fig. 6, the results clearly suggest that there was significant effect of the carbon dots with light exposure on the P. pastoris cells. The reduction of growth rate affected with light significantly was observed for the CDs concentration higher than 10 mg/ml and light exposure time more than 15 min. In the largest CDs concentration of 25 mg/ml and maximum exposure time of 60 min, there was ~ 54% fewer cells than in control condition. In comparison from the results were obtained in dark condition (Fig. 2), there were ~ 79% viable cells at the 25 mg/ml concentration of CDs. similar study show that photo induced antibacterial activity of carbon dots obtained from carbonization of glucose and poly(ethyleneimine). it was demonstrated that prepared CDs can be activated under even ambient room lighting condition (Meziani et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring intracellular ROS in the presence and absence of light
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 indicates the effects of CDs on ROS level as an oxidative stress biomarker. As it can be seen, administration of CDs concentrations in the absence of light exposure, showed considerable increment in cytosolic ROS level and CDs with higher concentrations induced more ROS generation. In addition, the ROS level in the cells exposed to the light showed a significant increase relation to un-exposed cells. In concentration of 25 mg/ml, the most increment of ROS can be detected at 60 min light exposure compared to the all groups. Finally, the ability of light- induced ROS generation of CDs may also be responsible for the growth inhibition of yeast cells (Fig. 4).

In the present study, toxicity assessment carbon dots synthesized from citric acid were evaluated on yeast cells Pichia pastoris. Results show that blue synthesized CDs revealed dose-dependent toxicity to yeast cells. The CDs binding curve confirmed the interaction between CDs and yeast cell surface. SEM images showed that the CDs caused cell shrinkage and hole formation on the surface of yeast cells, and also induces slightly cell deformation. Additionally, it was demonstrated that CDs could generate the ROS dose-dependently. Finally, results showed the growth inhibition and ROS generation effects of CDs were enhanced at light exposure, as an important environmental factor.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them especially Department of Toxicology and Pharmacology at Tehran University of Medical Sciences for ROS and Mitochondria activity test. The financial costs of this project have been covered by vice–presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University, G.C.
There are no conflicts to declare.
